title: Prompt: Opening Line
description: Generates a first line to steal, subvert, and continue.
entries: The map was wrong, and that was the good news.
By the time I understood the warning, I had already ignored it twice.
They buried the wrong man on Tuesday, and only I knew it.
My grandmother left me three things: a house, a debt, and a name I was never to say aloud.
The knock came at the hour we had agreed no one would ever come.
I have told this story four times, and each time a different person dies.
The town had a rule about the lake, and the rule had a reason nobody would give.
She arrived the way weather does, with no apology and no plan to stay.
It is easy to keep a secret when you have forgotten you are keeping it.
The last thing my father taught me was how to lie to a machine.
There were two of everything in the house, except us.
The letter said to burn it after reading, so of course I read it twice.
Nobody in the family agreed on how the fire started, which is how I knew it was set.
I was hired to find a woman who did not want to be found and paid to fail.
The child spoke first, and everyone in the room went pale.
We were three days out to sea before I noticed the crew never slept.
The clock in the hall had been wrong for so long it was almost right again.
I inherited my mother's hands, her temper, and one enemy she never named.
The door was locked from the inside, and I was the only one with a key.
Everyone remembers where they were the day the birds stopped singing.
He came home smelling of a season it had not yet become.
The first lie was small; the town built everything else on top of it.
I found the photograph before I found the reason to be afraid of it.
You could tell the honest people in that town by how little they said.
The garden was beautiful, which should have been the first clue.
She asked me to promise, and I did, before I understood what I was promising.
The road home was shorter than I remembered, and that frightened me most.
On the morning of the wedding, the groom's chair sat empty and warm.
We agreed never to speak of it, which is the surest way to think of nothing else.
The stranger knew my name, my street, and the thing I had told no one.
